elements.sent-2411-001: With the fast-growing penetration of power inverter-interfaced renewable generation, power systems face significant challenges in maintaining power balance and the nominal frequency.
elements.sent-2411-002: This paper studies the grid-level coordinated control of a mix of grid-forming (GFM) and grid-following (GFL) inverter-based resources (IBRs) for power system frequency regulation at scale.
elements.sent-2411-003: Specifically, a fully distributed optimal frequency control algorithm is proposed by leveraging the projected primal-dual gradient method and the structure of the physical system dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-004: This algorithm 1) restores the nominal frequency, 2) minimizes the total control cost, 3) respects the IBR power limits and the line thermal constraints, and 4) is implemented in a distributed fashion that only needs local measurement and local communication.
elements.sent-2411-005: The effectiveness and optimality of the proposed algorithm are demonstrated through high-fidelity electromagnetic transient (EMT) simulations on the IEEE 39-bus system.
elements.sent-2411-006: The widespread integration of power inverter-interfaced renewable energy resources, such as photovoltaic (PV) panels and wind turbines, has introduced emerging challenges to power system frequency control.
elements.sent-2411-007: Unlike traditional synchronous generators, inverter-based resources (IBRs) exhibit lower system inertia and faster dynamic behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-008: As a result, renewable generation uncertainties, frequent power fluctuations, and fast system dynamics present significant challenges to the classic three-level frequency control architecture \cite{Rebours2007Jan in power systems.}
elements.sent-2411-009: In particular, the traditional secondary and tertiary frequency controls, typically operating on timescales of minutes to hours, become inadequate for promptly restoring frequency and ensuring economic efficiency in IBR-rich systems.
elements.sent-2411-010: Nevertheless, the proliferation of IBRs also brings new opportunities, as they have fast response capabilities and remarkable power flexibility, which can greatly enhance power system operation when effectively coordinated.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, coordinating a huge number of IBRs for frequency control is challenging in practice, due to substantial communication and computational demands associated with centralized control schemes.
elements.sent-2411-012: Moreover, the dynamic models of IBR units are usually unknown to grid operators, as IBR control mechanisms are proprietary and not disclosed by manufacturers.
elements.sent-2411-013: In addition, real-time system disturbance information is often unavailable, which further complicates the grid-level coordination of IBRs.
elements.sent-2411-014: Hence, it is essential to develop advanced control strategies that enable distributed coordination of IBRs to enhance scalability and reduce reliance on detailed system model information.
elements.sent-2411-015: For power system frequency regulation, most existing work is devoted to the control of synchronous generators.
elements.sent-2411-016: In \cite{Li2015Jul , a distributed generator control scheme is proposed to incorporate economic dispatch into automatic generation control (AGC) to achieve faster and economical frequency regulation.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Recent studies extend it to the distributed control of flexible loads for optimal primary \cite{Zhao2014Jan and secondary frequency regulation \cite{Chen2020Sep,Mallada2017Jun .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Additionally, references \cite{WANG2020104607,10488734 address the issue of non-smooth control cost functions using the Clarke generalized gradient and the proximal approach.}
elements.sent-2411-019: These methods above focus on controlling synchronous generators or flexible loads, while the coordination of massive IBRs with fast response and distinct internal dynamics for frequency regulation is still under development.
elements.sent-2411-020: The primary control mechanisms of inverters can be mainly categorized as grid-forming (GFM) and grid-following (GFL) \cite{Du2020Aug .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Among other methods, droop control, virtual synchronous machine (VSM) control, and dispatchable virtual oscillator control (dVOC) are widely used in GFL and GFM inverters; see \cite{Pogaku2007Mar,Johnson2022Jan for detailed introductions.}
elements.sent-2411-022: In this paper, we study the grid-level coordinated control of a mix of GFM and GFL IBRs for power system frequency regulation.
elements.sent-2411-023: By leveraging the projected primal-dual gradient method and the physical inverter control dynamics structures, we develop a fully distributed optimal frequency control algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-024: Specifically, the algorithm controls the power setpoints of IBRs, and unifies the key features of primary, secondary, and tertiary frequency control.
elements.sent-2411-025: The main contributions of this paper are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-026: The proposed algorithm can achieve grid-level optimal coordination of IBRs for frequency control, which can restore the nominal system frequency, minimize the total control cost, and satisfy the power capacity limits of IBR units and line thermal capacity constraints.
elements.sent-2411-027: The proposed algorithm is implemented in a fully distributed fashion that only needs local measurement and local communication with neighbors, which significantly enhances the control scalability and preserves the privacy of individual IBR units.
elements.sent-2411-028: By utilizing the real-time measurements as system feedback, the proposed algorithm does not require the information of real-time disturbances.
elements.sent-2411-029: Moreover, we develop a high-fidelity, 100\% inverter-based, electromagnetic transient (EMT) model of the IEEE 39-bus system in MATLAB Simulink to validate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm via extensive simulations.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Distributed Optimal IBRs Control Algorithm} \label{alg:distributed_optimal_control} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE Input: Initial values of variables; parameters $(B_{ij})_{j:ij\in\sE}$ of the connected lines for each bus $i\in\sN$. \FOR{each bus $i \in \mathcal{N}$ in parallel} \STATE Measure local frequency deviation $\omega_i$ and power flows $P_{ij}$ of connected lines $ij\in\sE$; exchange $(\psi_i,\mu_i)$ with neighbor buses $j: ij \in \mathcal{E}$ via local communication. \STATE GFM IBR: update $P_{M,i}^s$ according to \eqref{eq: PMs} and execute control; update $(\mu^M_i,\nu^M_i)$ according to \eqref{eq:rM}, \eqref{eq:dr}. GFL IBR: update $P_{L,i}^s$ according to \eqref{eq: PLs} and execute control; update $(\mu^L_i,\nu^L_i)$ according to \eqref{eq:rL}, \eqref{eq:dr}. \STATE Update $(\psi_i,\sigma_{ij}^+,\sigma_{ij}^-)$ according to \eqref{eq:psi}-\eqref{eq:sigma-}. \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-088: In this paper, we propose a fully distributed control algorithm for coordinating large-scale GFM/GFL IBRs to achieve grid-level optimal frequency regulation.
elements.sent-2411-089: By leveraging the structure of the primary control dynamics of IBRs, we interpret the projected primal-dual gradient dynamics used to solve the reformulated OFC model as a combination of the physical grid dynamics and the proposed control algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-090: As a result, the control algorithm enables distributed implementation and outsources substantial computations to the physical system.
elements.sent-2411-091: High-fidelity EMT simulations demonstrate that the proposed algorithm can optimally restore the nominal frequency following a step disturbance and maintain the system frequency close to the nominal value under continuous disturbances.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
elements.sent-2411-092: The large-scale integration of inverter-interfaced renewable energy sources presents significant challenges to maintaining power balance and nominal frequency in modern power systems.
elements.sent-2411-093: This paper studies grid-level coordinated control of grid-forming (GFM) and grid-following (GFL) inverter-based resources (IBRs) for scalable and optimal frequency control.
elements.sent-2411-094: We propose a fully distributed optimal frequency control algorithm based on the projected primal-dual gradient method and by leveraging the structure of the underlying physical system dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-095: The proposed algorithm i) restores the nominal system frequency while minimizing total control cost and enforcing IBR power capacity limits and line thermal constraints, and ii) operates in a distributed manner that only needs local measurements and neighbor-to-neighbor communication.
elements.sent-2411-096: In particular, when the line thermal constraints are disregarded, the proposed algorithm admits a fully local implementation that requires no communication, while still ensuring optimality and satisfying IBR power capacity limits.
elements.sent-2411-097: We establish the global asymptotic convergence of the algorithm using Lyapunov stability analysis.
elements.sent-2411-098: The effectiveness and optimality of the proposed algorithms are validated through high-fidelity, 100\% inverter-based electromagnetic transient (EMT) simulations on the IEEE 39-bus system.
elements.sent-2411-099: Unlike conventional synchronous generators, inverter-based resources (IBRs) exhibit lower system inertia and faster dynamic behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-100: As a result, renewable generation uncertainties \cite{chen2017robust , frequent power fluctuations, and fast system dynamics present significant challenges to the classic three-level frequency control architecture \cite{Rebours2007Jan in power grids.}}
elements.sent-2411-101: In particular, traditional secondary and tertiary frequency controls, typically operating on timescales of minutes to hours, become inadequate for promptly restoring frequency and ensuring economic efficiency in IBR-rich systems.
elements.sent-2411-102: Nevertheless, large-scale IBRs also bring new opportunities, as their fast response and high power flexibility can substantially enhance grid operation when properly coordinated \cite{chen2019aggregate .}
elements.sent-2411-103: However, coordinating a huge number of IBRs for frequency control is challenging in practice, due to the heavy communication and computational burdens inherent in centralized control schemes\cite{Lyu2024Dec,Shen2023Oct .}
elements.sent-2411-104: Moreover, the dynamic models of IBR units are typically unknown to grid operators, as their control mechanisms are proprietary and not disclosed by manufacturers.
elements.sent-2411-105: These limitations highlight the urgent need for advanced control strategies that enable distributed coordination of IBRs to enhance scalability and reduce reliance on detailed system model information.
elements.sent-2411-106: Most existing research on power system frequency control focuses on the control of synchronous generators.
elements.sent-2411-107: Reference \cite{Li2015Jul proposes a distributed generator control scheme that integrates economic dispatch with automatic generation control (AGC) to achieve fast and economical frequency regulation.}
elements.sent-2411-108: More recent studies have extended this framework to the distributed control of flexible loads for optimal primary \cite{Zhao2014Jan and secondary frequency control \cite{Chen2020Sep, Mallada2017Jun, Wang2018Aug .}}
elements.sent-2411-109: Further, the challenges posed by non-smooth control cost functions have been addressed in \cite{WANG2020104607,10488734 through the use of Clarke's generalized gradient and the proximal method.}
elements.sent-2411-110: Collaborative frequency control methods that coordinate generators and loads have also been proposed in \cite{Wu2024Sep and \cite{Cherukuri2016Dec , employing the similar framework as in \cite{Chen2020Sep .}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: Despite these advances, the coordinated control of large-scale IBRs, characterized by fast response times and distinct internal dynamics, is still under development.
elements.sent-2411-112: The primary control mechanisms for IBRs can be mainly categorized as grid-forming (GFM) and grid-following (GFL) \cite{Du2020Aug .}
elements.sent-2411-113: Among others, droop control, virtual synchronous machine control, and dispatchable virtual oscillator control are widely used for GFM inverters; see \cite{Pogaku2007Mar,Johnson2022Jan for detailed introductions.}
elements.sent-2411-114: In \cite{9779512 , a reinforcement learning-based neural network controller incorporating Lyapunov functions is designed for IBRs to optimize the primary frequency control while ensuring system stability.}
elements.sent-2411-115: In \cite{Li2022Nov , a distributed AGC method based on area control error and proportional-integral (PI) control is introduced.}
elements.sent-2411-116: While these approaches contribute to advancements in primary and secondary frequency control, they fall short in achieving global grid-level optimal control.
elements.sent-2411-117: References \cite{Cherukuri2016Dec,Xu2021Jun employ the idea of Laplacian non-smooth gradient to achieve grid-level control optimality.}
elements.sent-2411-118: A similar technique, known as the distributed averaging-based integral method, is applied in \cite{9869334 to enable distributed implementation.}
elements.sent-2411-119: References \cite{Xu2021Jun and \cite{Yi2016Dec follow a similar approach to \cite{Chen2020Sep using the projected primal-dual gradient method for control algorithm design.}}}
elements.sent-2411-120: However, all these studies consider a simplified problem setting that neglects network power flow and line thermal constraints.
elements.sent-2411-121: To enforce the power (or current) capacity limits of IBR units, dynamics projection methods, including the continuous global projection \cite{Xia2000Jul, Gao2003Mar and discontinuous tangent cone projection \cite{Feijer2010Dec, Cherukuri2016Jan , are widely used to project state variables onto their corresponding feasible sets.}}
elements.sent-2411-122: In \cite{8399490, Tang2019Mar , an augmented Lagrangian method is adopted to avoid discontinuities associated with the tangent cone projection but needs careful tuning of a hyper-penalty parameter.}
elements.sent-2411-123: In this work, we employ the global projection approach introduced in \cite{Gao2003Mar to avoid computing the tangent cone at each iteration and to eliminate the need for tuning additional hyperparameters.}
elements.sent-2411-124: Moreover, we extend the problem formulation and convergence analysis in \cite{Gao2003Mar, Chen2022Jun, Chen2020Sep to complex settings that involve both equality and inequality constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-125: This generalization is motivated by the optimal frequency control problem, where power flow equations and line thermal limits are typically modeled as equality and inequality constraints, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-126: By leveraging the projected primal-dual gradient dynamics method and the intrinsic physical dynamics of inverter control, we develop a fully distributed optimal frequency control algorithm for coordinating IBRs.
elements.sent-2411-127: Specifically, this algorithm dynamically adjusts the power setpoints of IBR units to achieve optimal secondary frequency control.
elements.sent-2411-128: The key contributions of this work are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-129: Particularly, the algorithm relies solely on real-time system measurements of frequency and line power flow and does not require information of real-time disturbances.
elements.sent-2411-130: The proposed algorithm is fully distributed, requiring only local measurements and neighbor-to-neighbor communication.
elements.sent-2411-131: Moreover, when the line thermal constraints are not considered, we further design a fully local version of the algorithm that does not need any communication while still ensuring system-level control optimality and IBR power capacity limits.
elements.sent-2411-132: These distributed and local implementations significantly enhance control scalability and preserve the privacy of individual IBR units.
elements.sent-2411-133: We establish the asymptotic convergence of the proposed algorithm to an optimal solution using Lyapunov stability analysis.
elements.sent-2411-134: To our knowledge, this is the first work that proves the global asymptotic stability of the globally projected primal-dual gradient dynamics for optimization problems with both equality and inequality constraints.
elements.sent-2411-135: Moreover, we develop a high-fidelity, 100\% inverter-based, electromagnetic transient (EMT) model of the IEEE 39-bus system in MATLAB Simulink to validate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithms via extensive simulations.
elements.sent-2411-136: In this section, we first present the dynamic models of GFM and GFL inverters, along with the power network model.

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Distributed Optimal IBRs Control Algorithm} \label{alg:distributed_optimal_control} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE Input: Initial values of variables; parameters $(B_{ij})_{j:ij\in\sE}$ of the connected lines for each bus $i\!\in\!\sN$. \FOR{each bus $i \in \mathcal{N}$ in parallel} \STATE Measure local frequency deviation $\omega_i$ and power flows $P_{ij}$ of connected lines $ij\in\sE$; exchange $(\psi_i,\mu_i)$ with neighbor buses $j: ij \in \mathcal{E}$ via local communication. \STATE GFM IBR: update $P_{\tM,i}^\tr$ according to \eqref{eq: PMs} and execute control; update $(\mu^\tM_i,\nu^\tM_i)$ according to \eqref{eq:rM}, \eqref{eq:dr}. GFL IBR: update $P_{\tL,i}^\tr$ according to \eqref{eq: PLs} and execute control; update $(\mu^\tL_i,\nu^\tL_i)$ according to \eqref{eq:rL}, \eqref{eq:dr}. \STATE Update $(\psi_i,\sigma_{ij}^+,\sigma_{ij}^-)$ according to \eqref{eq:psi}-\eqref{eq:sigma-}. \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-294: By leveraging the structure of the primary control dynamics of IBRs, we interpret the projected primal-dual gradient dynamics for solving an optimal frequency control problem as a combination of the physical grid dynamics and the proposed control mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-295: This design outsources substantial computations to the physical system and enables distributed implementation that only needs local measurement and local communication with neighbors.
elements.sent-2411-296: Furthermore, when line thermal constraints are not considered, the algorithm supports fully local implementation without any communication while still ensuring control optimality.
elements.sent-2411-297: Using Lyapunov stability analysis, we prove that the proposed algorithm globally asymptotically converges to the optimal solution.
